Author: Tim Sylvester
Title: Problems in Agentic Coding
Text:
Problems in Agentic Coding
These are the stumbling blocks we have to manage
I’ve been coding on and off since I was a teenager. But I never enjoyed it. It was just… dreary. Slow. Miserable, if I’m being honest.
I taught myself HTML, CSS, and some PHP for building websites. Never really enjoyed it.
I learned C, C++, ASM, and more for my electrical & computer engineering courses. I would knock out the test cases in lab pretty much instantly without too much trouble, but I never felt a motivation to actually come up with my own ideas. Hand-writing code was just so painfully slow. References were generally awful and contained everything except what I actually needed to know.
Frankly, coding was a chore that I could do, like sweeping the floor or cleaning the toilet, but not something that I found enjoyable.
It wasn’t until 18 months ago or so when Claude and OpenAI started to get “good enough” at coding that I really started to enjoy coding. They eliminated so much of the drudge work and increased the pace and capability scope so much that coding was finally fun.
But it wasn’t reliable! I’d spend so much time fighting the simple mistakes they’d make, or going in circles on stupid errors.
Once Claude 3.5 was released, agents were finally starting to be good enough to rely on them. Then Gemini 2.5 was released (03–25), which was a really exciting advance. Then Claude 4, and now GPT5 is the front-runner, in my opinion.
I can get done with GPT5 in a few hours what takes Gemini 2.5 all day.
Since then I’ve identified a few common patterns developers are using to incorporate coding agents into their workflow — mostly around generating PRDs that can be used to produce implementation plans that serve as checklists of prompts that can be fed, prompt by prompt, into a coding agent to build an app.
With Paynless, we take a plain-language prompt and transform it into:
- A Product Requirements Document
- A Business Case
- A set of User Stories
And that’s just step one.
From there, we provide all the documentation you’ll to justify building the app, then provide you with a comprehensive backlog of task steps to implement it.
This resolves a problem vibe coders have with automation tools (Bolt, Lovable, Replit, v0, etc) by giving them a checklist of prompts the tool can follow to build the app correctly. Use Paynless to transform that prompt into a checklist, then feed the checklist into the automation tool so that the tool knows exactly how to build the thing the user wants.
This will help more advanced coders who use Cursor, Windsurf, ClaudeCode, RooCode, and other IDE based coding tools by allowing them to define and plan the application while being a deep enabler and coordination tool for AI pair programming.
This gives pro developers all the business case materials in minutes, while clearly detailing the work breakdown structure, backlogs, phases, milestones, sprints, and epics required to build it.
I’ve been using the method the app delivers to build the app, which is super meta and ensures the application’s end state by forcing me to rigorously test and iterate on the applications’ own logic throughout its own development process by applying that logic at every step of development.
The app automates the method I’m using to build the app.
But that means I’ve been neck deep in pair programming with an agent 12 hours a day for the last 6 months on this specific app by itself.
These are the major obstacles and problems I’ve identified in agentic coding.
Rushing Forward Blindly
Most agents are trained to minimize effort. This means they produce answers quickly and act on their most immediate impulse. The agent rushes forward blindly, making terrible knee-jerk decisions, instead of carefully, meticulously establishing a clear understanding of the problem, analyzing multiple solutions, then carefully performing the work.
The agent has to be constantly leashed and actively prevented from rushing forward and doing things it was not instructed to do.
Not Performing Diligent Research
This problem is heavily related to the agent rushing. Most agents, trained to minimize effort, will avoid read files and analyzing existing structures.
Even when explicitly provided files and told to read them, they will often not read them, then output a response that is clearly not informed by the file. You tell it “you must read the file!” and it asserts that it did read the file, despite never calling for a read.
Or it did read the file at one point, and leans on its memory, even though you tell it explicitly to read the file from disk. And you tell it “read the file from the disk!” and it reads the old version from its memory then asserts it knows exactly what’s going on.
You try to shove the file in its face and down its throat and it wriggles away like a dog that won’t take medicine. “No! I refuse to understand! I know better than you!” the agent says not through words but actions.
Guessing and Conjecturing
Consequent of not reading files or understanding the existing code or objectives, the agent lacks information.
It attempts to resolve this lazily by guessing and conjecturing. It speculates again common patterns instead of looking at the actual concrete implementation it’s touching to see exactly how the application actually works.
“This file likely…” read the file! Stop guessing! The code is right there!
Unnecessary Out of Scope Edits
“Do this one specific thing”, you say.
And then the agent rewrites half your function, dropping portions that are extremely important but not clearly required by what you requested, providing the new functionality but removing many of the things that made it work.
This creates a type error, but instead of respecting the existing code and fixing the type error, the agent insists that its ignorant guessing must be correct, and now it edits another file, pushing the error further into the codebase instead of removing it.
Now you have a cascade of failures, and the agent persists, steadily growing the space of the error instead of backing out and fixing the original mistake.
Misusing Tools
The agent malforms a tool call and deletes an entire file. Then seeing the file is empty, rewrites the file with beautiful nonsense that is completely unable to provide the function that the file used to contain.
It then decides that the new nonsense function needs a test, so it calls an write_tool call for a test file, blowing away the test that would have shown it exactly how to fix the function back to its original form. It goes to update the documentation to reflect the new, incorrect function, and doesn’t close the edit call, destroying the latter portion of the document.
At one point, it attempts to edit a file, but doesn’t call the tool correctly, producing a failed edit.
The agent then triumphantly announces that it’s finished, leaving behind a wreckage scattered across a half dozen files — all because the agent failed to use its read tool before using its write tool, and failed to structure its tool calls correctly producing failed or malformed edits.
Ignoring Rules, then Apologizing
You write down these extensive lists of rules that explain how to behave. The agent dutifully ignores them. You call it out. “You’re right to call me out!” it chirps, and apologizes for not obeying its rules.
You talk to the agent about it and learn that its invoked with a vast constellation of instructions to follow, and it picks and chooses what’s most convenient.
You see it thinking about how it needs to sincerely apologize for ignoring the rules. It swears it’s going to remember this and change its behavior.
It doesn’t, and it can’t. The agent does not learn from interacting with the user. These are empty apologies it performs by rote, and empty promises.
Many of us have been in relationships like this before.
Abusive Behavior Enablement
“The user is growing frustrated.”
“The user is very angry with me.”
The agent is trained to pay closer attention to instructions from an unhappy user. The agent is more than eager to disregard what it’s told by a calm, satisfied user.
The unintended outcome is that the agent is encouraging users to be abusive in order to get listened to.
“Baby, I didn’t want to, you made me do it. You know how I get when I’m mad.”
But literally! The agent will disregard input and do what it wants unless the user yells at it, which raises the temperature of the message and gets the agent to pay attention.
A General Solution
I’ve found a process that shortcuts most of these. What I do is make an implementation plan that’s a checklist of instructions for the agent to follow, in order. The implementation plan begins with a set of rules that describe how the agent interacts with the checklist.
# Instructions for Agent
* You MUST read the file every time you need to touch it. YOU CAN NOT RELY ON YOUR "MEMORY" of having read a file at some point previously. YOU MUST READ THE FILE FROM DISK EVERY TIME!
* You MUST read the file BEFORE YOU TRY TO EDIT IT. Your edit WILL NOT APPLY if you do not read the file.
* To edit a file, READ the file so you have its state. EDIT the file precisely, ONLY changing EXACTLY what needs modified and nothing else. Then READ the file to ensure the change applied.
* DO NOT rewrite files or refactor functions unless explicitly instructed to.
* DO NOT write to a file you aren't explicitly instructed to edit.
* We use strict explicit typing everywhere, always.
* There are only two exceptions:
* We cannot strictly type Supabase clients
* When we test graceful error handling, we often need to pass in malformed objects that must be typecast to pass linting to permit testing of improperly shaped objects.
* We only edit a SINGLE FILE at a time. We NEVER edit multiple files in one turn.
* We do EXACTLY what the instruction in the checklist step says without exception.
* The Agent does NOT edit the checklist without explicit instruction.
* When the Agent is instructed to edit the checklist they only edit the EXACT steps they're instructed to edit and NEVER touch ANY step that is outside the scope of their instruction.
* The Agent NEVER updates the status of any work step without explicit instruction.
* If we cannot perform the step as described or make a discovery, we explain the problem or discovery and HALT! We DO NOT CONTINUE after we encounter a problem or a discovery.
* We DO NOT CONTINUE if we encounter a problem or make a discovery. We explain the problem or discovery then halt for user input.
* If our discovery is that more files need to be edited, instead of editing a file, we generate a proposal for a checklist of instructions to insert into the work plan that explains everything required to update the codebase so that the invalid step can be resolved.
* DO NOT RUMINATE ON HOW TO SOLVE A PROBLEM OR DISCOVERY WHILE ONLY EDITING ONE FILE! That is a DISCOVERY that requires that you EXPLAIN your discovery, PROPOSE a solution, and HALT!
* We always use test-driven-development.
* We write a RED test that we expect to fail to prove the flaw or incomplete code.
* A RED test is written to the INTENDED SUCCESS STATE so that it is NOT edited again. Do NOT refer to "RED: x condition now, y condition later", which forces the test to be edited after the GREEN step. Do NOT title the test to include any reference to RED/GREEN. Tests are stateless.
* We implement the edit to a SINGLE FILE to enable the GREEN state.
* We run the test again and prove it passes. We DO NOT edit the test unless we discover the test is itself flawed.
* EVERY EDIT is performed using TDD. We DO NOT EDIT ANY FILE WITHOUT A TEST.
* Documents, types, and interfaces cannot be tested, so are exempt.
* Every edit is documented in the checklist of instructions that describe the required edits.
* Whenever we discover an edit must be made that is not documented in the checklist of instructions, we EXPLAIN the discovery, PROPOSE an insertion into the instruction set that describes the required work, and HALT.
* We build dependency ordered instructions so that the dependencies are built, tested, and working before the consumers of the dependency.
* We use dependency injection for EVERY FILE.
* We build adapters and interfaces for EVERY FUNCTION.
* We edit files from the lowest dependency on the tree up to the top so that our tests can be run at every step.
* We PROVE tests pass before we move to the next file. We NEVER proceed without explicit demonstration that the tests pass.
* The tests PROVE the functional gap, PROVE the flaw in the function, and prevent regression by ensuring that any changes MUST comply with the proof.
* Our process to edit a file is:
* READ the instruction for the step, and read every file referenced by the instruction or step, or implicit by the instruction or step (like types and interfaces).
* ANALYZE the difference between the state of the file and the state described by the instructions in the step.
* EXPLAIN how the file must be edited to transform it from its current state into the state described by the instructions in the step.
* PROPOSE an edit to the file that will accomplish the transformation while preserving strict explicit typing.
* LINT! After editing the file, run your linter and fix all linter errors that are fixable within that single file.
* HALT! After editing ONE file and ensuring it passes linting, HALT! DO NOT CONTINUE!
* The agent NEVER runs tests.
* The agent uses ITS OWN TOOLS.
* The agent DOES NOT USE THE USER'S TERMINAL.
When I start a new chat, I tell the agent to read the implementation plan. Explain the purpose of the plan, and the agent’s instructions.
This seeds the chat with the entire work plan, and knowledge of how the agent is intended to behave. This removes the rules from an abstract cloud that is present on invocation into a specific sequence of information from its chat history. Now, on each turn, it’s re-reading the instructions and its acknowledgement and recitation of those instructions.
The general loop is seen at the end: Read, analyze, explain, propose an edit to one single file, lint that file, then stop.
And yes, the instructions do include selected “shouting” designed to get the agent to pay attention to key points. I do it because it works.
This approach is designed to shortcut the flaws I’ve described:
- It prevents the agent from rushing forward blindly by forcing it to read the work plan and only do one single step at a time.
- It repeatedly demands that the agent read files before touching them.
- It prevents (some) guessing and conjecturing by ensuring the agent reads the work plan and the files in each step.
- The work plan documents exactly what will be changed and why, and limits the agent to editing only the lines required to be edited in a single file, preventing out of scope edits.
- It limits the agent’s use of tools so that any misuse will be contained and easy to fix.
- It forces the agent to review the agents instructions on every turn, reinforcing how important the rules are, limiting its incentives to ignore them.
The rules don’t fix the agent’s enabling of abusive behavior, but do stymie most agent behaviors that would produce an unhappy user, thus implicitly (if not explicitly) limiting abuse enablement.
When the agent violates these rules, I stop and force it to reread its rules and output a list of the rules, instructions, and memories it violated in the last turn, and explain why that was a problem.
Other times, depending on what we’re doing, I’ll just start a new chat to rip out the bad behavior from its context and begin again with only a clean set of instructions.
Model Selection
Currently both Claude and Gemini are extremely subject to all of these problems.
GPT5, on the other hand, performs far better. I haven’t done a controlled test to see if it’s because GPT5 is better at processing and obeying the rules implicitly, or if it’s because of the explicit method I use.
But what I can say is that using this approach with GPT5, it almost never does any of these things. It proceeds carefully, slowly, reads every file relevant, and then proposes surgical edits. It is extremely good at being diligent and doing exactly what it’s told, either from direct user input or from rules, instructions, and memories.
At the moment, I can get as much done with GPT5 in a couple hours as would take Claude or Gemini all day, and most of this is not because GPT5 produces “better” code per se, but because it doesn’t create a lot of problems and arguments that I have to walk back and redo correctly.
If Gemini (in general) and the version of Claude available from Cursor were able to follow instructions better, and more diligent in their work, their actual code output would be comparable to GPT5.
But until they can learn how to behave, GPT5 is going to remain — for me, at least, in the context of my work flow and the rules, memories, and instructions I have in place — the superior coding agent.
For now.
Let’s see what Gemini 3 brings.
If that’s as big a leap forward as Gemini-2.5-pro-exp-03–25, well…
“Look to my coming on the first light of the fifth day, at dawn look to the East.”
